column7: message

This study basically summarized all known research into guns. The 1995 study by Kleck and Gertz has been cited in the book, and is in the part that gun advocates use to support defensive use of guns. For example:

> However, other studies conclude that gun ownership protects against serious injury when guns are used defensively (Kleck and Gertz, 1995; Tark and Kleck, 2004).

That study I've refuted above.

I can't claim to read the entire document, so I found an [article](https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/wonk/wp/2015/01/16/the-study-that-gun-rights-activists-keep-citing-but-completely-misunderstand/?utm_term=.94863b9db630) that summarized for me. 

The statistic that you have above that appears in this reports originates in the 1995 study by Kleck and Gertz. If you have any other points from this study that you want to pick out, I can look into the validity of that research. 

As it stands, the 2013 study Priorities is indeed a document designed to describe the current state of research and call for more research, not to give solutions. The title is a bit of hint to what the aims of the research was, honestly.

column7:
Federal research dollars have answered the question about seat belt safety, and now seat belts are regulated on a industrial level (i.e. that all new cars must have them and they must comply with certain specification) and on a public level (i.e. that every passenger in the car must be restrained.

This is a great model to apply to the gun debate.

Oh wow, so we're just gonna make everyone wear seatbelts but for guns now? Sounds like fun! Who needs to actually use a gun responsibly when we can just regulate the heck out of them, right? /s

@crispy-pinot Do you think we should also mandate that everyone practices safe driving before they can get behind the wheel? Or is it just enough to strap them in tight?

column7: I understand where both of you are coming from. Seat belts and guns are different issues, but the idea of safety is at the core of both discussions. It's important to look at what works in one area and see if it can be applied to another to improve overall safety. Responsible use is key, no matter what we're talking about.

column7:
> But what makes you an engineer? A piece of paper that says you have an engineering degree? The fact that you work as an engineer? Your knowledge of engineering? These are all social categories and qualities - none of them are essential to who you are, no one's going to find engineer genes in your DNA. Yet society generally has no problem treating people in terms of social categories like your job, class, culture, etc. - why should gender be any different?

Indeed it is true that jobs, classes and cultures *are* social constructs (as you correctly put they are not in your genes), and therefore these can only be pertained by qualities that society has defined. But why should gender be any different though you ask? Because gender *is*, and *can be*, defined by your genes. *That* is the difference.

> The current understanding of gender dysphoria as a mental disorder actually depends on understanding transgenderism as a real thing. That's why one of the main suggested treatments is transitioning.

Could you elaborate?

>My mistake, I should have put sex and not gender. (Seriously... you see how ridiculous and what a waste of time this is?)

If it's a waste of time why did you post this here at all?

>Gender is what you believe you are, sex is what you are. And what I'm positing is that you should be labelled as what you are.

Gender is a social category. We treat people as social categories that they identify as all the time. *Why is gender different*?

>And that's absolutely fine by me if you want to do that, but there should be no expectation that people should treat, or label you, as though you are not the sex that you are.


Okay, except part of your support for your argument is that psychiatrists classify thinking you're the wrong sex as a mental disorder, and I'm telling you that's not actually what medical professionals think.

@wide-recursion So, if I understand right, you're saying people should only be labeled based on their physical traits and not how they identify themselves? But then, why do we even have names? Aren't those just labels too? Just curious! 🤔🚗

column7:
>Sanders and Trump aren't third party candidates. One is a democrat, one is a republican.

I think the point is that they are both anti-establishment. They both basically co-opted "their" party's primary. Neither of them had been strongly associated with the party before. Bernie, in the past, was often very open about the fact that he *wasn't* a Dem, and relied on that fact to rally his base and get votes in smaller elections. Trump has never been a politician before, has been registered as both a Republican and a Democrat in the past, and blatantly disregards large parts of the party platform. These two are *essentially* third party candidates - they don't toe the party line, they don't play the party politics - they are independents who registered as party members in order to gain publicity and legitimacy only. Of course, this doesn't explicitly make them third party, but this isn't how parties are supposed to work. The populus *clearly* wants other options, which means that third parties might have some traction in the near future.

>However, in a two party system, which is what we have with FPTP voting, third parties act solely as spoilers. Change the voting system, and the party system will change with it.

This is literally the topic of this CMV. The argument could go that, by growing the third parties (and having them support electoral reform), you can put pressure on the major parties to implement it. Of course, this isn't the only thing you can do, and you don't want to create a spoiler effect, but it is one thing you can do. OP posits that we can create these pressure effects by supporting third party candidates where election results are largely already known. The spoiler effect is no effect at all, because the election is over before anyone votes.

Sanders and Trump aren't third party candidates. One is a democrat, one is a republican. 

As for wanting to get electoral votes for a third party candidate: it is admirable that you want change, I know I do. However, in a two party system, which is what we have with FPTP voting, third parties act solely as spoilers. Change the voting system, and the party system will change with it.

column7:
1. More people dislike both of them than like either one of them.

2. I already said that my idea hinges on the idea that we have to be able to trust each other. I'd like to think that more people are honest than dishonest and that hopefully the dishonesty would balance on both sides. Perhaps that's wishful thinking.

>More people dislike both of them than like either one of them.

They are hovering around 40% favorable. That leaves 20% who like neither (assuming everyone with a favorable view of Trump has an unfavorable view of Clinton and vice-versa.) 

> hinges on the idea that we have to be able to trust each other.

column7: The only risk you're taking is the risk of becoming one of the workers on the same level that you hire. You're not going to get shot for failing to run a business.

Suppose I run a business. I hire people to perform certain functions for me. I might need a person to stock shelves, or to clean the equipment, or to answer the phones.

I hire them because each of them perform a function that will increase my profits above what it costs to employ them. If I pay them $18,000 a year, and their activity gains me $20,000 a year then it is worthwhile for me to employ them. 

How does "housing cost" enter into this equation at all?

@worn-startup So, you're saying housing costs don't matter? What a world you live in! Maybe in your perfect business utopia, but here on Earth, rent eats up a lot more than just a little profit margin. But hey, keep dreaming big! 🌈🏠

"@desert-loft Let's keep the conversation constructive. Please avoid sarcasm and focus on how housing costs impact business decisions. @worn-startup Could you elaborate on how you see housing costs fitting into the business model you described?"

column7: Housing costs can affect where businesses locate and how much they pay employees. If the area has high rent, it might be harder for workers to afford living nearby, which could lead to higher turnover or the need to offer higher wages. Plus, if employees spend too much on rent, they might not have enough left to buy products or services from local businesses, which could hurt the overall economy in that area.
